frameworks.name: fitness-maximizing encoding
frameworks.one_line: sensory codes shaped to maximize survival-relevant outcomes rather than reconstruction accuracy

frameworks.name: information capacity-constrained deep neural networks
frameworks.one_line: capacity-limited DNNs trained on the same task as humans reproduce fitness-maximizing codes

frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: accuracy-maximizing sensory representation as default expectation that the paper contrasts with

frameworks.name: normative cross-disciplinary framework
frameworks.one_line: unified account combining neuroscience, computer science, and economics

core_question: Are early sensory representations adapted to maximize fitness rather than to maximize encoding accuracy?
key_assumptions: sensory encoding has biological capacity limits
behavioural goals can shape the optimization criterion for sensory codes
fitness consequences can be operationalized in a controlled task
manipulations: behavioural goals tied to differing fitness payoffs
training capacity-constrained DNNs on the same task as humans
novel object-perception goals probed with fMRI
key_findings: humans flexibly adapt sensory encoding strategies to promote fitness maximization
capacity-constrained DNNs trained on the same task reproduce this pattern
novel behavioural goals induce efficient stimulus representations in early sensory areas in fMRI
interpretations: fitness-maximizing rules imposed by the environment apply at early stages of sensory processing in humans and machines
accurate sensory representation is not necessarily what biological systems optimize
